html:
Campine N.V. (SQ0.F / CAMB) - Current Price About EUR 219

Campine is no longer just a small Belgian lead recycler. It is now the world’s leading antimony trioxide producer, the only company Campine identifies as recycling ATO directly from waste streams, and one of Europe’s more strategically relevant circular metals platforms. The market is still treating the 2025 profit explosion as a one-off commodity spike. That is partly right, but not right enough.

The stock closed around EUR 219 on May 15, 2026, implying roughly EUR 329 million of equity value on 1.5 million shares. Campine earned EUR 56.6 million in 2025, or EUR 37.75 per share, and proposed a EUR 10 dividend. Reported EBITDA was EUR 89.1 million, but about EUR 9.5 million came from Ecobat bargain-purchase accounting, so the cleaner 2025 EBITDA base is closer to EUR 80 million. Even after adjusting for that, the stock is around 6.5x adjusted earnings and roughly 4x clean 2025 EBITDA, before allowing for the full-year impact of Ecobat.

The easy bear argument is that 2025 was peak antimony. It was. Antimony metal peaked near USD 60,000/t in mid-2025 and Campine’s own annual report says prices closed 2025 at USD 34,750/t, versus a USD 52,078/t average for the year. Current-cycle pricing is lower again: SMM showed May 15, 2026 Europe antimony around USD 27,500/t and Europe ATO around USD 28,000/t. That means nobody should value Campine on a repeat of first-half 2025 economics.

But the real thesis is not “2025 repeats.” It is “the new floor is much higher than the old floor.” China’s export controls did not merely create a temporary price spike; they changed buyer behavior. Reuters reported that China suspended its outright U.S. ban through November 27, 2026, but exporters still need Beijing licenses and the ban on exports to U.S. military users remains. Campine also said in December 2025 that China had not resumed exports of antimony compounds, including ATO, despite earlier indications of relaxation. That distinction matters. Raw antimony metal supply is loosening through Southeast Asian smelters, but ATO remains a more concentrated bottleneck.

This is the variant perception: Campine is not primarily a mined-antimony beta. It is a Western processing, recycling, formulation, and customer-qualification asset in a market where security of supply suddenly matters. Fastmarkets says antimony metal supply is more diversified, but ATO production remains concentrated, with limited near-term non-Chinese growth. Campine sits exactly where the bottleneck is.

The business has also changed below the headline antimony story. The Ecobat France acquisition closed in October 2025 and added two battery recycling sites plus Le Plomb Francais, a downstream lead products business serving medical, nuclear, construction, zinc, and copper customers. The acquired assets generated about EUR 100 million of revenue and around EUR 5 million of average EBITDA in recent years, but Campine paid only about EUR 14 million subject to adjustments. More important than the starting EBITDA is the industrial fit: 70,000 tonnes of added battery recycling capacity, available smelting capacity, polypropylene feedstock for recycled plastics, and the opportunity to adapt furnaces to recover antimony, tin, and other metals.

That makes Campine less fragile than a pure ATO squeeze stock. The 2025 segment split was EUR 52.4 million of Specialty Chemicals EBITDA and EUR 27.3 million of Circular Metals EBITDA. Specialty will step down with lower antimony prices. Circular Metals should step up from a full year of Ecobat, synergies, higher utilization, and downstream lead products. The market sees falling antimony prices and stops. The better read is that one segment normalizes downward while the other segment gets structurally larger.

The balance sheet is also better than it looks after a year of huge working-capital swings. At year-end 2025, Campine had EUR 17.6 million of cash, EUR 13.5 million of bank loans, and modest lease liabilities. Pro forma for the proposed EUR 15 million dividend, it will move from slight net cash to modest net debt, but leverage is still trivial relative to normalized EBITDA. Free cash flow was EUR 29 million in 2025 despite heavy metal-price working capital, capex, and the acquisition. If antimony prices stabilize rather than spike again, working capital should become less hostile.

The stock is still obscure. The controlling shareholder, F.W. Hempel Metallurgical GmbH, owns 71.86%, leaving a small free float. Liquidity is better after the move to Euronext Brussels continuous trading and Small Cap index inclusion, but this remains a tiny, underowned name. Retail discussion exists on Reddit, but it looks scattered and low-conviction rather than euphoric. That is useful: there is enough narrative for discovery, not enough sponsorship for crowding.

My forward base case uses current-cycle antimony economics, not 2025 peak pricing. I assume Specialty Chemicals EBITDA falls from EUR 52 million to roughly EUR 32-35 million as ATO prices normalize, but remains far above 2024 because Campine has retained share, non-Chinese sourcing relationships, and a better export customer base. I assume Circular Metals rises to roughly EUR 28-32 million with a full Ecobat year and early synergies. That gets to EUR 60-65 million of forward EBITDA, EUR 34-39 million of net income, and around EUR 23-26 of EPS. At EUR 219, that is only 8-9.5x forward earnings and around 5-5.5x forward EBITDA for a strategic Western bottleneck with a clean balance sheet.

The bear case is real: ATO substitution, Southeast Asian supply, Chinese licensing normalization, and customer destocking could compress Specialty margins faster than Ecobat can offset. This is not a compounder that deserves 15x EBITDA. It is a small, controlled, illiquid, commodity-linked processor. But the downside case now has more asset support than the old Campine did, because Circular Metals is larger and the company owns scarce permits, furnaces, customer approvals, and recycling know-how.

The upside case is that investors eventually stop valuing Campine as a one-year commodity accident and start valuing it as a critical-materials processor with two durable earnings engines. If EUR 60 million of EBITDA is the new mid-cycle instead of EUR 25-30 million, the stock is still too cheap. If long-term ATO agreements, defense-sensitive sourcing, third-generation antimony recycling, and Ecobat synergies push normalized EBITDA back toward EUR 75-85 million, the equity torque is large because the share count is only 1.5 million.

Bear target: EUR 150, down 32%. This assumes antimony spreads fully normalize, China resumes enough ATO exports to remove the scarcity premium, Specialty EBITDA falls near 2024 levels, and group EBITDA settles around EUR 35-40 million. The balance sheet and Circular Metals assets prevent a deeper collapse.

Base target: EUR 300, up 37%. This assumes EUR 60-65 million of forward EBITDA, around EUR 35-38 million of net income, and a still-discounted 7.5x EBITDA multiple for a small controlled company. The EUR 10 proposed dividend adds another 4.6% near-term cash yield if approved and paid.

Bull target: EUR 430, up 96%. This assumes ATO remains structurally tight, Campine converts customer diversification into longer contracts, Ecobat synergies land, third-generation antimony recycling gains credibility ahead of 2027 startup, and normalized EBITDA is closer to EUR 80 million. At only 8x EBITDA, that supports an equity value around EUR 640 million, or roughly EUR 430 per share.
